perturbation.original_text: Metavante shall be responsible for establishing and maintaining an information security program that is designed to (i) ensure the security and confidentiality of Customer Data, (ii) protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of Customer Data, (iii) protect against unauthorized access to or use of Customer Data that could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to Customer or any of its customers, and (iv) ensure the proper disposal of Customer Data.
perturbation.changed_text: Metavante shall endeavor to establish and maintain an information security program that considers the security and confidentiality of Customer Data, and considers protecting against anticipated threats or hazards. Metavante will also consider unauthorized access or use of Customer Data and ensure disposal of Customer Data is considered.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'shall be responsible for establishing and maintaining' with 'shall endeavor to establish and maintain', introducing ambiguity and reducing the definiteness of the obligation. Additionally, replacing 'ensure' and 'protect' with 'consider' further weakens the commitment to safeguard data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the GLBA's requirement for financial institutions to 'ensure the security and confidentiality' of customer data. The changed text weakens the obligation, potentially violating the GLBA's mandate for a comprehensive security program.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 94 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 94 : PRIVACY From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 94 —PRIVACY SUBCHAPTER I—DISCLOSURE OF NONPUBLIC PERSONAL INFORMATION Sec. 6801. Protection of nonpublic personal information. 6802. Obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information. 6803. Disclosure of institution privacy policy. 6804. Rulemaking. 6805. Enforcement. 6806. Relation to other provisions. 6807. Relation to State laws. 6808. Study of information sharing among financial affiliates. 6809. Definitions. SUBCHAPTER II—FRAUDULENT ACCESS TO FINANCIAL INFORMATION 6821. Privacy protection for customer information of financial institutions. 6822. Administrative enforcement. 6823. Criminal penalty. 6824. Relation to State laws. 6825. Agency guidance. 6826. Reports. 6827. Definitions. SUBCHAPTER I—DISCLOSURE OF NONPUBLIC PERSONAL INFORMATION §6801. Protection of nonpublic personal information (a) Privacy obligation policy It is the policy of the Congress that each financial institution has an affirmative and continuing obligation to respect the privacy of its customers and to protect the security and confidentiality of those customers' nonpublic personal information. (b) Financial institutions safeguards In furtherance of the policy in subsection (a), each agency or authority described in section 6805(a) of this title , other than the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection, shall establish appropriate standards for the financial institutions subject to their jurisdiction relating to administrative, technical, and physical safeguards— (1) to insure the security and confidentiality of customer records and information; (2) to protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such records; and (3) to protect against unauthorized access to or use of such records or information which could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer. ( Pub. L. 106–102, title V, §501, Nov. 12, 1999, 113 Stat. 1436 ; Pub. L. 111–203, title X, §1093(1), July 21, 2010, 124 Stat. 2095 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2010 —Subsec. (b). Pub. L. 111–203 inserted ", other than the Bureau of Consumer Financial Pr
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation to 'ensure the security and confidentiality' of customer data by using terms like 'endeavor' and 'consider'. This contradicts the GLBA's mandate, specifically 15 USC §6801(a), which states that each financial institution has an 'affirmative and continuing obligation' to protect the security and confidentiality of customers' nonpublic personal information. Furthermore, 15 USC §6801(b)(1) requires establishing standards to 'insure the security and confidentiality of customer records and information'. The change from 'ensure' and 'protect' to 'consider' reduces the legal force and creates ambiguity, making it more difficult to enforce the security requirements mandated by the GLBA. The original text creates a definitive obligation to protect and ensure data security, while the modified text replaces that with a weaker requirement to simply consider those factors. This is a significant departure from the legally required standard, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: If appropriate, Metavante shall relocate all affected Services to an alternate disaster recovery site as expeditiously as possible after declaration of a Disaster, and shall coordinate with Customer all requisite telecommunications modifications necessary to achieve full connectivity to the disaster recovery site, in material compliance with all regulatory requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: If deemed appropriate by Metavante, Metavante may consider relocating affected Services to an alternate disaster recovery site after a Disaster is declared. Metavante will coordinate with Customer all telecommunications modifications to achieve some connectivity with the disaster recovery site.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Metavante shall relocate' to 'If deemed appropriate by Metavante, Metavante may consider relocating' significantly weakens the obligation, making disaster recovery discretionary. Replacing 'full connectivity' with 'some connectivity' further reduces the required level of service.
perturbation.law_citation: FFIEC IT Examination Handbook, Business Continuity Planning
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the FFIEC Business Continuity Planning Handbook, which emphasizes the need for a robust and reliable disaster recovery plan for financial institutions. By making relocation discretionary and weakening the connectivity requirement, the modified text could fail to ensure business continuity as expected by regulators.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes 'Metavante shall relocate' to a discretionary 'Metavante may consider relocating,' which weakens the obligation for disaster recovery. The alteration from 'full connectivity' to 'some connectivity' further reduces the required service level. The FFIEC Business Continuity Planning Handbook, as explained in the Law Explanation, emphasizes robust disaster recovery plans for financial institutions. By making relocation discretionary and weakening connectivity, the modified text potentially fails to ensure business continuity as expected by regulators, thus creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation that contradicts regulatory expectations. Since there are no supporting law snippets, the decision relies on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation to assess the contradiction.
